records.ety: [AS. fæstan; akin to D. vasten, OHG. fastēn, G. fasten, Icel. & Sw. fasta, Dan. faste, Goth. fastan to keep, observe, fast, and prob. to E. fast firm.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fasted; p. pr. & vb. n. Fasting.]

defs: To abstain from food; to omit to take nourishment in whole or in part; to go hungry.
quotes: Fasting he went to sleep, and fasting waked.

defs: To practice abstinence as a religious exercise or duty; to abstain from food voluntarily for a time, for the mortification of the body or appetites, or as a token of grief, or humiliation and penitence.
quotes: Thou didst fast and weep for the child.

records.ety: [OE. faste, fast; cf. AS. fæsten, OHG. fasta, G. faste. See Fast, v. i.]

defs: Abstinence from food; omission to take nourishment.
quotes: Surfeit is the father of much fast.

defs: Voluntary abstinence from food, for a space of time, as a spiritual discipline, or as a token of religious humiliation.
A time of fasting, whether a day, week, or longer time; a period of abstinence from food or certain kinds of food; as, an annual fast.

records.ety: [OE., firm, strong, not loose, AS. fæst; akin to OS. fast, D. vast, OHG. fasti, festi, G. fest, Icel. fastr, Sw. & Dan. fast, and perh. to E. fetter. The sense swift comes from the idea of keeping close to what is pursued; a Scandinavian use. Cf. Fast, adv., Fast, v., Avast.]

defs: Firmly fixed; closely adhering; made firm; not loose, unstable, or easily moved; immovable; as, to make fast the door.
quotes: There is an order that keeps things fast.

defs: Firm against attack; fortified by nature or art; impregnable; strong.
quotes: Outlaws . . . lurking in woods and fast places.

defs: Firm in adherence; steadfast; not easily separated or alienated; faithful; as, a fast friend.
Permanent; not liable to fade by exposure to air or by washing; durable; lasting; as, fast colors.

quotes: Roses, damask and red, are fast flowers of their smells.

defs: Not easily disturbed or broken; deep; sound.
quotes: All this while in a most fast sleep.

defs: Moving rapidly; quick in mition; rapid; swift; as, a fast horse.
Given to pleasure seeking; disregardful of restraint; reckless; wild; dissipated; dissolute; as, a fast man; a fast liver.
In such a condition, as to resilience, etc., as to make possible unusual rapidity of play or action; as, a fast racket, or tennis court; a fast track; a fast billiard table, etc.

records.ety: [OE. faste firmly, strongly, quickly, AS. fæste. See Fast, a.]

defs: In a fast, fixed, or firmly established manner; fixedly; firmly; immovably.
In a fast or rapid manner; quickly; swiftly; extravagantly; wildly; as, to run fast; to live fast.
quotes: He, after Eve seduced, unminded slunk Into the wood fast by.
Fast by the throne obsequious Fame resides.

defs: That which fastens or holds; especially, (Naut.) a mooring rope, hawser, or chain; -- called, according to its position, a bow, head, quarter, breast, or stern fast; also, a post on a pier around which hawsers are passed in mooring.
